dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for a chest pain call near Seventh Street.
Hello, I'm Alex with Medical Rescue. You called 911 for chest pain-how long have you been feeling this?
The trouble breathing started around 2 a.m., and it's gotten worse.
Daisy, can you tell me your full name and what date it is today?
My name is Daisy. I think we're in June.
What day, and are you in [CITY], Daisy?
June tenth, yes, I'm sitting on my couch in [CITY].
Ms. Daisy, is anything in your throat making it hard to breathe?
I'm breathing fast, and it's tight in my chest.
You're breathing at a very fast rate-34 times per minute.
Heart rate is very high, 130 beats per minute.
Your blood pressure is 164 over 84.
Tell us more about the trouble breathing you've had today.
I'm having increasing trouble breathing with some wheezing.
She used multiple nebulizer treatments and inhalers this morning, but nothing helped her.
Are you taking your usual medications today?
I take Albuterol, Atrovent, and Pepcid, but it's hard to stay on them since my breathing is so bad.
Your oxygen level is 100% currently.
What medications are you allergic to?
Penicillin and Naprosyn.
Daisy, you're alert and aware-oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
You're very sweaty, which is a common sign during intense respiratory effort.
Have you experienced any pain or stiffness in your head or neck?
I have some back pain, usually, but not today.
Your breath sounds are low in both lungs-left and right.
You have a history of Coronary Artery Disease, Primary Hypertension, COPD, GERD, and past Embolism. You also have Anxiety and Chronic Back Pain.
You also take Warfarin regularly, right?
Our glucose reading is zero.
Given your COPD exacerbation, we will follow the COPD management protocol next.
I'll begin with a four-lead ECG to monitor your heart now.
I'll now take a 12-lead EKG for a more detailed look at your heart rhythm.
I will administer a subcutaneous dose of Terbutaline to help relieve your breathing.
Based on your symptoms and response to treatment, [COMPANY_NAME] is the best hospital for you.
Your pulse is now at 135, your breathing rate is 24, and you're breathing more steadily.
Your oxygen saturation has dropped slightly to 99%, but is otherwise stable.
The EKG now shows sinus tachycardia, no other acute issues-this suggests improvement.
I'll securely fasten your stretcher now for a smooth transport.
Don't worry, Daisy-we'll be with you every step of the way.
You have no nuchal rigidity and no signs of head trauma.
Your pupils are appropriately reacting to light.
You started the call clearly diaphoretic, which indicates the body is working hard to manage your breathing.
Your skin is warm and pink, showing good perfusion despite your respiratory distress.
Your breath sounds are significantly decreased in both the left and right lungs-suggesting airway obstruction.
We've treated you for a COPD exacerbation, and with a history of Embolism, we need to ensure timely hospital evaluation.
Your respiratory rate at 34 signifies tachypnea, a clear sign of respiratory distress.
You've had this worsening for hours despite standard relief treatments.
Your pupils are equal and reactive, and there is no nuchal rigidity noted.
You seem in more comfortable sitting upright-this is your preferred position for airway maintenance.
Your systolic blood pressure is over 160-this is consistent with your history of hypertension.
We've pre-set the stretcher to allow you to continue sitting, since this is your most comfortable position.
Though you appear pale, your skin color improves with oxygen administration.
Your history of Hypertension and CAD needs to be mentioned to the hospital team upon arrival.
Your breath sounds are significantly improved after the second dose, now showing moderate wheeze bilaterally.
First dose of 0.25 mg Terbutaline has helped, and we note some return of wheeze bilaterally.
Now on high-flow oxygen, your saturation is holding steady at 99%.
You are the top priority for [COMPANY_NAME] to further assess for cause of respiratory distress.
Your heart rate remains elevated, consistent with sinus tachycardia-monitoring ongoing.
Repeat 0.25 mg Terbutaline subcutaneous now given as per protocol.
After baseline, second dose began showing expected improvement in ventilatory effort.
Your skin is dry aside from perspiration but shows no blanching.
When was the last time you had anything to eat or drink?
Tried to eat breakfast around 6 a.m., but couldn't finish due to the breathing.
You're maintaining stable blood pressure-164 over 84-plenty of perfusion to vital organs.
Please keep your allergies marked for the hospital in your chart since you have Penicillin and Naprosyn.
You're currently taking famotidine (Pepcid) as well, correct?
No signs of jugular venous prominence or heart failure contributing to your respiratory issue today.
You also have a history of Vascular Thrombosis-important to mention to the hospital team.
Your wheezing is moderate and bilateral after the second dose of Terbutaline-clear sign of improvement.
No change-glucose still reads zero but patient chart shows no indication of diabetes.
All buckles and devices set, patient transfer progress underway, drivers ready now.
Second vitals trended toward stability since first check-supporting a good medication sequence.
No obvious nasal obstruction from inspection. You also report no recent throat pain.
You remain angled to breathe easier from baseline posture due to your airway challenges.
No change-glucose still reads zero, and patient data confirms no diabetic condition.
All secured and turnout under observation. RN will meet us in emergency.
Your systolic blood pressure still over 160-consistent with your hypertensive baseline.
You remain paler from puffing efforts, showing no worsening color changes upon repeats.
Your peripheral pulses are palpable and strong despite pulmonary challenges.
No signs of asymmetric breath sounds reported, indicating no localized obstructive event.
Second dose of medication trended toward stability, continuing good sequence.
No signs of blocked airflow or acute airway threat. Work of breathing remains spiked.
Since you responded well to two Terbutaline doses and EKG remains stable, [COMPANY_NAME] is the right move.
Blood pressure still normal for baseline, no hemodynamic crisis seen.
Your breathing difficulty began in the early morning-no other precipitating factors noted.
All secured and turnout under observation. RN will meet us in emergency.
Take one more round of vitals then ready for RCC call prior hosp.
You remain maintained from baseline, watching for color as arrival nears.
No change-glucose still reads zero, yet patient is not diabetic.
Your breathing difficulty coincided with morning activities-no other activities involved on onset.
All secured and turnout under observation. RN will meet us.
Focus remains on both lung sound response and treatment trend.
Airway remains open and uninhibited, no compromise noted at any point yet.
No perfusion risk or hypotension flags-hospital arrival primed.
Further meds not scheduled yet, readiness maintained.
Daisy breathing okay, steadily now, prior hospital prep done.
No change in vitals or visual signs, protocol adhesion solid prior transport.
Stability maintained through arrival prep steps.
Diaphoresis evident but within limits, management unchanged.
Oxygen levels optimized throughout, no new signs of desaturation in this case.
Start of trouble noted at 2 a.m. only, no escalation beyond initial chest effort evident.
Pulse trend steady, heart rate showing baseline tracking, no spikes.
Hospital course set to [COMPANY_NAME], protocols followed thread.
Status unchanged on last review, no new skin issues evident.
Allergy details rechecked and passed to staff at later handoff.
Transition equip set, oxygenation maintained.
All pulses show non-critical levels, as of now.
Treatment limit held at two doses, patient response adequate to warrant compliance from here.
All in place, patient transfer in process, drivers chilled for move.
Full data sent, meds and vts passed correctly at arriving points.
[COMPANY_NAME] on alert, incident status given, en route setup at go signal.
Last recheck shows stability with no incident pre move.
Occupancy full on stretcher, stable pass to station.
Second round of report given all key medical facts at ED.
Load safe and position kept for transit for patient comfort now.
O2 confirmed on board, all steps to protect maintained.
Therapies complete, no further needed due to reported good effect.
All transport risks mitigated, no unplanned step taken.
All parties good to roll onward with patient and standard.
Nurse on floor aware on arrival, with coded status for care.
Exit under ten clean, setup safe from scene as SOP.
Entire vehicle dedicated to patient, only core staff aboard.
No action needed on the run, patient steady and aware.
Final patient sign out complete on arrival pace with nurse.
All taken down,-safe drop as transit complete.
No further tasks planned for this transport mission point.
Board clean, no bag left behind, loaded up and clear.
Breath rate remains evened, all motion signs captured through.
Procedures and meds well Ventilated, delivery clean.
Response team complete deliver process with full care seen.
Skin complete check, no drama on new signs of trouble.
Unload ready now, descending back into scene for next.
Read for hand-off note for nurse, full coding and steps taken.
Move away rapid per command, no delays or hold.
Time over EMT actions, finished smooth with no press.
End shift clean, no backlog, vehicle cleared for next step.
Complete transport, no glitch in flow or patient well being.
Last O2 read over at 99% final in ED intake.
No back-plan, [COMPANY_NAME] direct, full track followed for event.
Sojourn correctly timetabled with full pass to care team.
Device removed, unit clear for next, ready for next use.
All steps adhered, nothing more to handle on board.
